meta.title: The Nations This Morning
meta.headline: The live economic question is not whether major projects move. It is who has authority to say yes, no, or not yet.

throughLine: The strongest signal in the past 24 hours came from Ottawa, where First Nations chiefs narrowed the Assembly of First Nations' role on major projects to national advocacy and insisted that Canada deal directly with affected rights and title holders. That turns the federal major-projects push from a permitting story into a governance and capital-allocation story.

oneThing.headline: Chiefs draw a boundary around the AFN's role in major-project talks
paragraphs: At the Assembly of First Nations annual general assembly in Ottawa, chiefs passed resolutions on Wednesday opposing federal or provincial fast-tracking that would dilute First Nations rights or decision-making. The key amendment said the AFN cannot act as the national representative or coordinator for project-specific negotiations, approvals or implementation.

APTN reported that chiefs framed the AFN's role as national advocacy and coordination only. Canada, in their wording, must keep meeting its constitutional obligations directly with affected First Nations rights and title holders, Treaty Nations and organizations.

Economically, this matters because Ottawa's Major Projects Office and the October First Ministers meeting are being positioned around infrastructure, resources, trade corridors and energy. The vote does not stop any project. It clarifies that speed, financing and market access still run through Nation-specific consent, accommodation and environmental review.
oneThing.whyItMatters: Capital can price permitting risk; it has a harder time pricing unclear authority. The AGA vote is a reminder that national Indigenous advocacy can support a project framework, but it cannot substitute for the jurisdiction of the Nations whose lands, waters and treaty rights are affected.
unknowns: Which projects Ottawa will put first through the Major Projects Office.
Who will attend the October First Ministers meeting on behalf of First Nations, and how that delegation will be chosen.
Whether federal timelines will be adjusted when affected Nations need more time, funding or information.
oneThing.nextCatalyst: Federal ministers were scheduled to address chiefs on Thursday, July 16, including on the economy and natural resources development.

developments.headline: Federal ministers face chiefs after the major-projects vote
developments.whatHappened: The Canadian Press, carried by APTN, reported that Indigenous Services Minister Mandy Gull-Masty, U.S. Trade Minister Dominic LeBlanc, Public Safety Minister Gary Anandasangaree and Natural Resources Minister Tim Hodgson were expected to address the AGA on Thursday. APTN said chiefs were debating 53 resolutions, including major projects, child welfare, Indian Act status rules and Vatican decrees.
developments.whyItMatters: The ministerial lineup puts trade, public safety, natural resources and community services in the same room as the major-projects debate. That is the operating intersection for federal economic policy: project approvals, public infrastructure, rights recognition, emergency capacity and social-service fiscal obligations are not separate lanes for many Nations.
developments.whatRemainsUnknown: Whether ministers will offer process changes, money, timelines or only political assurances.
developments.nextCatalyst: Public readouts from the final day of the AGA and any posted AFN resolution package.

developments.headline: Clean-water legislation remains an infrastructure and rights test
developments.whatHappened: APTN reported strong criticism at the AGA of Bill C-37, the First Nations Clean Water Act. Speakers argued the bill falls short of the 2021 settlement obligation to pass drinking-water legislation, and Parliamentary Secretary Ginette Lavack did not commit to restoring the language chiefs wanted around the inherent right to safe drinking water.
developments.whyItMatters: Water is a rights issue, but it is also a hard infrastructure file. Communities under long-term boil-water advisories need capital, operations funding, enforceable standards and accountable delivery. The unresolved question is whether the bill creates a financeable, rights-consistent framework or another engagement process with no firm service timeline.
developments.whatRemainsUnknown: Whether the government will amend the bill before committee and how any final law will be funded.
developments.nextCatalyst: Committee-stage language and any formal AFN direction coming out of the AGA.

developments.headline: Child-welfare reform is becoming a fiscal-design fight
developments.whatHappened: Cindy Blackstock told chiefs that Canada should support the First Nations-led Loving Justice plan rather than advance its own plan for long-term First Nations child and family services reform outside Ontario. APTN reported that the Canadian Human Rights Tribunal approved an Ontario-specific deal in March 2026, while the broader national reform remains before the Tribunal.
developments.whyItMatters: The economic stakes are not abstract. The rejected 2024 national agreement was valued at $47.8 billion, and the Ontario agreement was reported at $8 billion. The current dispute is about the structure of funding, accountability and whether a permanent backstop exists until better data is available.
developments.whatRemainsUnknown: The Tribunal timetable and whether Canada will voluntarily align with the First Nations-led plan.
developments.nextCatalyst: Any Tribunal scheduling order or federal response to the Loving Justice plan.

developments.headline: No fresh B.C. transaction cleared the 24-hour bar; live project-risk files stay on the ledger
developments.whatHappened: The B.C. scan found no new, verified ownership, financing or procurement transaction published in the past 24 hours. The live B.C. economic-risk files remain Tsleil-Waututh Nation's judicial review of Burrard Inlet dredging permits, B.C.'s approval path for the Mount Polley tailings-dam increase over Xat'sull First Nation concerns, and planning for new Indigenous-led conservation areas in Qat'muk, the Skagit Headwaters and the Raush Valley.
developments.whyItMatters: These are different economic signals: port capacity and tanker loading, mine-life extension and tailings risk, and land-use planning that can limit extraction while supporting stewardship economies. None is a completed transaction today, but each affects how capital, regulators and Nations read B.C. project certainty.
developments.whatRemainsUnknown: Court schedules, permit-amendment timing, and the fall engagement process for the proposed conservation areas.
developments.nextCatalyst: Judicial-review filings, B.C. Mines Act permit amendments, and the province's fall conservation engagement.

whatHappensNext: Watch for the final AGA resolution package and any public ministerial commitments from July 16.
Track whether Ottawa names a first tranche of Major Projects Office files and whether affected Nations publish their own positions.
Keep Bill C-37 and the Loving Justice plan on the ledger because both convert rights disputes into fiscal and infrastructure decisions.
stillWatching: This morning's verified developments are First Nations-specific. No Inuit or Métis economic item cleared the materiality and verification bar in the scanned window.
The unverified LNG Canada storage-tank equity item surfaced through an aggregator but was not included because no accessible primary or independent source could be confirmed during this run.
Tsleil-Waututh Nation's Burrard Inlet dredging challenge remains a live B.C. port-capacity and consultation file.
Mount Polley remains active because B.C. approved a higher tailings dam while Xat'sull First Nation concerns and appeal language remain unresolved.
The proposed Qat'muk, Skagit Headwaters and Raush Valley conservation areas move next to detailed planning and fall engagement.
